assignment_description:
Introduction to Joshua
⍞ Materials: ESV Large Print Bible

➜ INTRO

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Intro to Joshua

➜ NOTEBOOK

Joshua Commissioned
⍞ Materials: ESV Large Print Bible; Notebook, Student Bible Atlas

➜ RECAP
What do you recall about the intro to Joshua from last time?

➜ INTRO
Before he died, Moses passed along the leadership of the people of Israel on to Joshua (Deut. 31:7-8). In today's reading, Joshua will speak to the people about entering the Promised Land - the nation of Canaan. As Joshua speaks, he reminds the people of what they should do to have success and blessing. He also says, "Be strong and courageous." As we read, count how many times you hear this phrase or words similar to this.

➜ READ, NARRATE & DISCUSS
Joshua 1:1-18

•How many times did you hear Joshua say "Be strong and courageous?" (4: Verses 6, 7, 9 & 18) Why do you think he repeats this? How do you imagine you would be feeling as a member of Israel about to go into this land you have been waiting to enter for so long?
•Based on verse 9, why should the people not be afraid and _why_ could they be strong and courageous? (vs.9 - "...for the Lord your God is with you...")
•What are the people to do in order to experience success in taking over the land? (See vs. 7 & 8)

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resource: Student Bible Atlas p.9 Map 4 and Map 5
1. At the end of Deuteronomy, we are told that before he died, Moses "went up from the plains of Moab to Mount Nebo" (Deut. 34:1) and was shown the Promised Land. On Map 4 (the larger map), locate Moab, the Jordan River (Labeled as "R. Jordan") and Canaan.
2. Look at the image in the bottom right-hand corner to see the view from Mount Nebo.
3. Looking at Map 5, locate Mt Nebo and the Jordan River. Follow the arrows to see the direction the Israelites will move forward in the book of Joshua. The Jordan River moves north to south and to the east is a plateau (mostly flat, level ground) and mountains to the left. The people are approaching Canaan from the south. The first move they will have to make is to cross the Jordan River.

➜ NOTEBOOK
In your notebook, create a title page for the book of Joshua.
Draw a picture of Joshua speaking to the Israelites as their new leader. As time and ability allow, write the phrase "Be strong and courageous." Older students might write a short summary of the passage, or a key verse from the passage.

Rahab Hides the Spies
⍞ Materials: ESV Large Print Bible; Manners & Customs of Bible Times

NOTE: Two different options are given for the reading from Manners & Customs. Choose which one best fits the age or the interest of your student(s).

➜ RECAP
What do you recall about what we read last time?

➜ INTRO
Joshua is going to send two spies into the land to check it out. Maybe he thought back to how he had once been a spy for Moses!

➜ READ, NARRATE & DISCUSS
Joshua 2:1-24
•Deuteronomy 28:10 says, "And all the peoples of the earth shall see that you are called by the name of the Lord, and they shall be afraid of you." How does what Rahab says and does agree with (or prove) this verse from Deteronomy? (If needed, see Josh. 2:9-11, 24)
 
➜ READ, NARRATE & DISCUSS
Rahab's family lived in a house that was part of the walls of Jericho. Read the following passage for an idea of what this may have looked like.
Manners & Customs "Brick Houses"
Younger students: View the illustrations and read p.28 "When the semi-nomadic"-"stone was white." p.30-31 "The flat roof"-"in Matthew 24:17."
Older students, or as time and interest allow: View the illustrations and read p.28-31 "When the semi-nomadic"-"in Matthew 24:17."

Crossing the Jordan
⍞ Materials: ESV Large Print Bible; Notebook

➜ RECAP
What do you recall about what we read in Joshua last time?

➜ READ, NARRATE & DISCUSS
Joshua 3:7-17; 4:15-24
•How would the stones help the children (or future generations) remember what happened at the Jordan River? If you were there and someone asked you what the stones were for, what would you answer?
•Look at verse 24 again. Why did God lead the Israelites across the Jordan River, as he had done for them at the Red Sea?

➜ NOTEBOOK
Draw a picture or write a short summary of today's passage. Older students may pick a key verse or phrase from today's reading to write as well.

The Walls Fall
⍞ Materials: ESV Large Print Bible; Notebook

➜ RECAP
What do you recall about what we read last time?

➜ READ, NARRATE & DISCUSS
1. Israel celebrates Passover and remembers how God had delivered them from Egypt
Read Joshua 5:10-15
•In verse 12, it says the manna stopped. Why was manna provided in the first place and who provided it? (Exodus 16)_ _Why do they no longer need the manna? (Joshua 5:11-12)
2\. Read Joshua 6:1-19
Jericho is the first city the Israelites need to conquer as Jericho protects the entry into the land from the east where they crossed the Jordan. Scholars say Jericho "was about a twelve-acre site...[and] Based on the size of the site today, it seems that it might have taken Joshua's army less than half an hour to march around the city walls...In the ancient world every war was holy war because people believed that their gods called them to war, led them in battle, and fought on their behalf...For this assault it was the ark and the priests that gave indication of God's presence with the Israelites and made it plain that the battle belonged to the Lord." (Bible Story Handbook, Walton) Usually when an army defeated a city or people, they would take the plunder or goods (such as jewelry and anything that was worth a lot). In this case, the people did not take anything, because it wasn't them that claimed the victory - it was God who won this battle.

➜ VIEW
∞ Video Link: Sound of the Shofar
View the video showing various Jewish people blowing the shofar, the ram's horn trumpet that was used in Israel's history - and is still used today to call people to worship. What would it have been like to hear this sound if you were part of the army marching around Jericho?

➜ NOTEBOOK
Draw a picture or write a short summary of what you read today. Older students might write out a key verses from this passage or write a longer summary.

Achan's Sin
⍞ Materials: ESV Large Print Bible; Manners & Customs

NOTE: The teacher may choose to either read out loud to the student(s) or summarize the reading from Manners & Customs which has to do with warfare and religion. If reading, the couple sentences that may not be appropriate may be skipped.

➜ RECAP
What do you recall about what we read last time?

➜ INTRO
Last time, we saw that the people did not take any of the plunder from Jericho as they were directed by God: "But all silver and gold, and every vessel of bronze and iron, are holy to the Lord; they shall go into the treasury of the Lord." (6:19) The word "plunder" means the valuables or possessions that would be taken from the people defeated in war. Armies who won battles would take the plunder to not only celebrate the victory, but to add the loot to their money pile so they could afford to keep battling! After the battle at Jericho, everyone followed this rule and listened...except Achan.

➜ READ & NARRATE
Joshua 7:1-25
"When Achan took goods as plunder, it was like making a claim that the Israelites had won the battle rather than the Lord."

➜ READ & DISCUSS
Manners & Customs of the Bible "War and religion" (See note above)
p.268-269 "It was not"-"his own people."

Preview Manners & Customs reading regarding warfare. Either summarize or skip the couple sentences that may not be appropriate for younger students.
assignment_teachersNote:
★ TEACHER NOTE
Preview verses 24 and 25 where Achan and his family are killed because of his disobedience.

assignment_description:
Tricked
⍞ Materials: ESV Large Print Bible

➜ RECAP
What do you recall about what we read last time?

➜ INTRO
Two events happen in today's passage. Do you recall what genre the book of Joshua is? (Historical narrative) In a narrative (story), events are told as though someone is asking, "And then what happened?"
First, Joshua renews the covenant that God had given to Moses. Do you remember what God promised Moses? (Deut.28 - if you follow and obey God, then you will be blessed. If you do not follow or obey God, then you will be cursed and things will not go well.)
As we read the story, we might ask, "And then what happened?"
Next, some kings in the land of Canaan come up with a sneaky plan to trick the Israelites. In this context a "king is the ruler of a single city," and these cities were starting to worry about the Israelites. Why do you think they were worried?

➜ READ & NARRATE
Joshua 8:30-9:15
•Why do you think it is good for Joshua to renew the covenant God had made with Moses and remind the people what God had said?
•The Gibeonites dressed in old clothes and brought stale food to try to give the impression that they had traveled from a faraway land. Unfortunately, Joshua and the Israelites "did not ask counsel from the Lord" (9:14). Now they are in a treaty with people from the land of Canaan. What do you think will happen?

The Sun Stands Still
⍞ Materials: ESV Large Print Bible; Notebook

➜ RECAP
What do you recall about what we read last time?

➜ INTRO
The Gibeonites have tricked Joshua, but he will remain faithful to the treaty he made with them. Five kings in the land of Canaan decide to come fight against the Gibeonites because now they are all getting worried at the strength of Gibeon and of the Israelites. Next the historical narrative book of Joshua will answer the question, "And then what happened?"

➜ READ & NARRATE
Joshua 9:16-21; 10:1-14

➜ VIEW & DISCUSS
∞ Image Link: Joshua Commanding the Sun to Stand Still Upon Gibeon
•What do you notice about this painting? Do you see the people and do you see Joshua? What do you notice in the sky?

➜ NOTEBOOK
Draw or write a short summary in your notebook about today's reading. Older students may chose a key verse or write a longer summary.

Conquering the Land
⍞ Materials: ESV Large Print Bible; Student Bible Atlas

NOTE: Today's reading will cover three chapters, but will skip verses for time's sake.

➜ RECAP
What do you recall about what we read last time?

➜ INTRO
Today's reading covers three chapters of information of Joshua and the Israelites defeating various kings in Canaan. These stories of Israel conquering the Promised Land point to God's might and power in fighting for his people, Israel. They are in covenant with him. Do you remember what the word "covenant" means? (A promise.) Because God _is_ faithful, he will _be_ faithful.

➜ READ, NARRATE & DISCUSS
Today's reading is in two parts:
1. Joshua 10:42-43 •Who did the fighting for Israel and what is happening as a result? (All the kings are being captured.)
2. Eventually Israel captures all the kings and they will have some rest from fighting. While there were many kings that were defeated, we will read just one of the lists of their names.
11:23; 12:7-24

➜ MAP
⍞ Map Resource: Student Bible Atlas p.10 Map 6
Look at the map and note the red dashed line. What does the map key state this red line shows? (Area permanently controlled by Israel.) Do you recognize any of the names of cities on the map that you have heard or read about?

Dividing the Land
⍞ Materials: ESV Large Print Bible; Student Bible Atlas; notebook

➜ RECAP
What do you recall about what we read last time?

➜ INTRO
Joshua chapter 13 all the way through chapter 21 lists out how Joshua divided up the land between the tribes of Israel. After all, the book of Joshua "shows how God kept his covenant promise to give the land to Israel. He prepared them to enter Canaan, sent his commander to lead them, provided aid in battles, distributed the land, and renewed the covenant. The division of land, though not much of a story, is an incredible climax of the process that had brought the Israelites from their slavery in Egypt to the land that God had promised to Abraham through the covenant made over four centuries earlier. This is fulfillment of covenant promise on a grand scale." (Bible Story Handbook)

➜ READ & NARRATE
Our reading will be in three parts:
1. Read Joshua 14:1-5
2. Read 16:1-10 Chapter 16 is an example of what these chapters list as far as boundaries of the inherited land that each tribe received. Other chapters define the boundary of the other tribes' lands.
3. Read Joshua 21:43-45 These verses give a summary of the purpose of the entire book of Joshua. They could be called the Key Verses or Main Verses of the book.
•How would you summarize the purpose of the book of Joshua according to these verses?

➜ READ
⍞ Map Resource: Student Bible Atlas, p.11 Map 7 "Dividing the Land"
Find the area that was given to the tribe of Ephraim on the map that you read about in chapter 16. Locate the Jordan River. Why would it be good to have your land inheritance touch water?

➜NOTEBOOK
As time and ability allow, copy down all or part of the verses as shown here that state the purpose of the book of Joshua. Younger students might copy down just one or two phrases, older students might copy down a few phrases or the entire selection. Alternatively, the student could draw a picture.

Joshua 21:43-35: "Thus the Lord gave to Israel all the land...And they took possession of it, and they settled there. And the Lord gave them rest on every side...The Lord had given all their enemies into their hands. Not one word of all the good promises that the Lord had made to the house of Israel had failed; all came to pass."

Joshua Gets Ready for Goodbye
⍞ Materials: ESV Large Print Bible; Notebook; Manners & Customs of Bible Times

➜ RECAP
What do you recall about what we read last time?

➜ INTRO
In the first verse of today's reading, Joshua states that he is now old. He gives the first of two speeches to the people of Israel. Imagine you are Joshua, what would you think would be important to tell the people in one of your last speeches you will ever be able to give them?

➜ READ & NARRATE
Joshua 23:1-15

➜ READ & NARRATE
Although the people were moving into a land where some cities had walls and homes built (as we read about Jericho), tents were also still used at this time. Read the passages on tents and dwellings, and view the images on p.25 about how a tent was erected.
Manners & Customs "Dwellings" p.20-21 "There are two"-""Ahab for this reason."
"The Tent" p.24-26 "The tent of"-"door of the leader."

➜ NOTEBOOK
Draw a picture or write a sentence about today's reading. Older students may draw, write a summary or copy down a key verse (or more!) from Joshua's speech.

Meeting at Shechem
⍞ Materials: ESV Large Print Bible; Notebook

➜ RECAP
What do you recall about what we read last time?

➜ INTRO
The first part of today's reading might make you think, "Hm, I feel like I've heard this before." God is speaking through Joshua to remind the people of Israel about their history. The story is given using the personal pronoun of "I", which is used 18 times in these verses. "I took...I gave...I sent...I plagued...I brought...I delivered, etc." Speaking in the first person (by repeatedly using the word "I") in the telling of the history, God reminds the people who won the battles thus far and who had brought them to where they are today as a people. "God reviewed the marvelous deeds he had performed for Israel's benefit...From first to last Israel's conquests, deliverances, and prosperity were because of God's good mercies and were not of their own making." (The Bible Knowledge Commentary)

"Now therefore" begins verse 14. The word "therefore" is an adverb that means "as a result", or "for that reason." In other words, the people had been reminded of God's leading, and now - as a result of that - they had a decision to make. Read the passage and see what it is they have to choose.

➜ READ & NARRATE
Joshua 24:1-15
•Who had brought the people of Israel to where they now stood? (God)
•Because God had brought them that far in their story (or history), what choice did they now need to make?
•What do you think their response will be? (We will find out what their actual response was in our next reading.)

➜ NOTEBOOK
Draw or write a summary of today's reading. Older students may write a longer summary or write down a key verse from the passage.
Alternative Option: As time allows, a short summary of the entire book of Joshua can be written or discussed out loud. Questions could include: What happened in Joshua? Were the people following God? What was Joshua like as a leader?

The Choice
⍞ Materials: ESV Large Print Bible; Notebook; Manners & Customs

➜ RECAP
What do you recall about what we read last time?

➜ INTRO
We are coming to the end of the book of Joshua. Overall, the people have obeyed and followed after God in the book of Joshua. And, according to Deuteronomy 28, what will be the result of following after God? (The people will receive blessings in the form of having land, fruitful crops, lots of kids or growth of their nation, and things will go well for them.) Did they receive blessings as Joshua has led them into Canaan? In what way(s)? (Conquered enemies, inherited actual land for their tribes, food to eat - no more manna, etc.)

➜ READ & NARRATE
Joshua 24:16-33

•The people respond right away to Joshua when he tells them they have a choice to make. How do they respond? (vs. 16-18)
However, Joshua "was not at all satisfied" with their answer and challenges them on it. Eventually, he tells them exactly what they need to do. "He had heard the pledge on their lips; now he challenged them to prove their sincerity by their works...There could be no mixing of allegiance to God with idol-worship. A firm choice had to be made then as in every generation." (Bible Knowledge Commentary) At the end of the passage, Joshua - as the final act of his leadership of Israel - renews the covenant between God and the people. God would bless them if they followed him, and they would be cursed if they did not follow him.

➜ READ & NARRATE
⍞ Materials: Manners & Customs p.82; 86 (Skip the pages of maps) "When the Jewish"-"...began to advance."

➜ NOTEBOOK
Draw a picture or write a short summary of Joshua renewing the covenant at Shechem. Older students may write down a key verse or write a longer summary of the back-and-forth of Joshua and the people.

Judges Begins
⍞ Materials: ESV Large Print Bible; Notebook

➜ RECAP
What do you recall about what we read last time?

➜ INTRO
While the Israelites followed God as they entered Canaan, there were little seeds of disobedience showing up throughout the book of Joshua. Now, in the book of Judges, unfortunately those seeds grow into full disobedience. The book tells the history of Israel over centuries, but the book also wanted to encourage the Israelites to obey and be faithful to God.

The word "judges" might remind us of a courtroom, however, at this time in history, the judges were "tribal or national leaders. Often they were military commanders." They would settle arguments and give advice to the people of their territory. "Since Israel had no king or other central political figure, the judges played an important role in Israel's survival." (Basic Bible Commentary)

Sadly, as the book continues on the Israelite's turn further and further away from God and things are very dark and rather disturbing. Throughout the book, certain judges will be introduced and we will focus on their stories.

➜ READ & NARRATE
Judges 1:27-36; 2:1-10
•One bad choice Israel made is not driving out the Canaanites completely and now things start to spiral downward. In 2:10 what does it say the new generation knew about what God had done for them? (Nothing.) How would you imagine Joshua (if he was alive at that time) would feel about this?

➜ NOTEBOOK
Turn to a new page to create a title page for the book of Judges. Write the title of the book, and what genre it is: Historical Narrative. Draw a picture or write a short summary of the passage. Older students may write a longer summary or write down key verse(s) of the passage, including 2:10.

Cycles
⍞ Materials: ESV Large Print Bible; Notebook

➜ RECAP
What do you recall about what we read last time?

➜ INTRO
The Israelites have started worshipping false idols and have turned way from following God. And now a cycle (or a series of events that keep repeating) will start that will happen throughout the book of Judges. "The cycles (disobedience, punishment, cry for help, deliverance) show how God demonstrated his power and mercy by delivering his people time after time after his justice had demanded that he bring punishment." (Bible Story Handbook) Sadly, it is also obvious that the leaders of the Israelites are just as bad as the people. In the cycle, the punishment they experienced was in the form of oppression, being conquered by an enemy or even being enslaved.

➜ READ & NARRATE
Judges 2:11-23

Othniel
⍞ Materials: ESV Large Print Bible; Notebook

NOTE: There are two options for the reading today. For the second option of reading (3:7-26), preview the passage as there is an instance of violence as King Eglon gets stabbed. However, the story is also a great example of the humanity included in the Bible.

➜ RECAP
What do you recall about what we read last time?

➜ INTRO
Today's reading introduces the first judge, Othniel. Recall that there are cycles in the book of Judges that involve disobedience, punishment, cry for help, deliverance. Each time that cycle happens, their decline into disobedience, idolatry and sin is even worse. As we read, listen for those steps in the cycle.

➜ READ & NARRATE
Younger Students: Judges 3:7-15
Older Students: Judges 3:7-26

➜ NOTEBOOK
∞ Image Link: Cycle of Judges
Draw and/or label the cycle that is in the book of Judges. You can come up with your own words or use the following for the steps of the cycle: disobedience, punishment, cry for help, deliverance. An example is given at the link (Your chart/cycle does not need to look exactly like this!) Older students can put verse numbers from today's reading about Othniel next to each step (I.e. Disobedience 3:7)

Deborah and Barak
⍞ Materials: ESV Large Print Bible; Manners & Customs

➜ RECAP
What do you recall about what we read last time?

➜INTRO
Today's reading will introduce us to the next judge in Israel's history. The judges "were often as bad as the people. Deborah was certainly an exception to this, as she was a faithful prophetess and brought deliverance in association with the commander Barak...Deborah was first and foremost a prophetess, one who brought God's word to the people. People came to her for decisions because she would inquire of God for them. It is likely that in this particular instance they came to ask God what they should do about the Canaanite armies. She responded by sending for Barak and giving him God's orders." (Bible Story Handbook)

➜ READ & NARRATE
Judges 4:1-15

➜ READ & NARRATE
Over the next couple lessons, we will read a few passages from Manners & Customs to learn more about how the armies and groups fought at this time of the Old Testament.

⍞ Materials: Manners & Customs
"The horse" p.213 "The horses of" - "back to Jezreel."
p.145 "Iron was originally"-"for the Temple."
Optional: As interest or time allows, the entire passage regarding workers with metal could be read. This gives a short history of the development of metal being used in the times of the Old Testament p.143-145 "workers of metal"

Deborah's Song
⍞ Materials: ESV Large Print Bible; Notebook

➜ RECAP
What do you recall about what we read last time?

➜ INTRO
Sisera went into battle confidently, knowing the Israelites were outnumbered and would seemingly be easily to conquer. But God used both human means (the swords of the Israelites) and divine to defeat the Canaanites. Today's passage is a victory hymn written by Deborah. In it, we see further information as to the divine means God used to crush Sisera by sending a sudden flood to the Kishon River.

➜ READ & NARRATE
Younger students: 5:1-15, 20-21
Older students: Judges 5:1-31
•In verses 20 and 21, torrent refers to a flood of waters that overtook the army. If you were driving in chariots, how would a sudden flood of a river slow you down?

➜ NOTEBOOK
Draw a picture of Deborah, Barak or the battle they fought against Sisera or write a short summary. Older students may write a longer summary, or copy key verses down.
Optional: Try writing a short victory song or poem celebrating the victory of Israel over Sisera.

Gideon
⍞ Materials: ESV Large Print Bible; Manners & Customs

➜ RECAP
What do you recall about what we read last time?

➜ INTRO
The Israelites experienced peace for 40 years under Deborah's judgeship (5:31b), but then their cycle of disobedience began again and they were conquered by the Midianites. Let's ask the question that the books of Historical Narrative answer: And then what happened?

➜ READ & NARRATE
Judges 6:1-4; 11-24
•How did Gideon respond when the angel of the Lord spoke to him?

➜ READ, VIEW & DISCUSS
∞ Image Link: Terebinth tree
1. View the image of the terebinth tree, a relative of the oak tree.
2. The Midianites were a semi-nomadic tribe, which means they traveled from place to place at times. So they liked to attack when the crops had been recently harvested (vs. 3-5).
Manners & Customs "The Gezer Calendar" and "Grain crops" p.86-90 (Skip p.88-89) "By the time"-"...for warmer days."
"Threshing" p.94 "The separation of" - "...to his enemies."
•Take a couple minutes to look at the schoolboy's calendar in the text box labeled "The Gezer Calendar" If you live in an area where crops
are grown, how does your area's calendar match this ancient calendar?

Gideon and Tests
⍞ Materials: ESV Large Print Bible; Notebook

➜ RECAP
What do you recall about what we read last time?

➜ INTRO
In today's reading there are a few tests that take place. First of all, Gideon uses "the dew and the fleece for an oracle. In an oracle, a yes-or-no question was posed to deity, and some mechanism was designated for deity to answer...Since God had already sent an angel to tell him that he was supposed to lead the armies into battle, Gideon wanted to check to see if those orders were still in place." Once the normal result happened, Gideon flipped it because he still doubted. "So with great apology (appropriate, since God had already given instruction), he then asked for the indicator to be switched; having the fleece dry and the threshing floor wet. This does indeed show a lack of faith on his part." (Bible Story Handbook) The second tests have to do with how the warriors drink from the water. Gideon once again was being asked to put his faith in God going before the Israelites to fight their enemies.

➜ READ & NARRATE
Judges 6:25-40; 7:1-11
(In order to keep the story together, today's reading is a little longer than usual. If additional time is needed to finish today's reading, the next lesson time can be used.)

➜ NOTEBOOK
Draw a picture or write a short summary of today's reading. Older students may write a longer summary or choose one or two main verses from the passage to copy into their notebooks.

Catch Up

➜ CATCH UP
Use this time to catch up on the reading in this or another course, or explore Extra Helpings.

Catch Up

➜ CATCH UP
Use this time to catch up on the reading in this or another course, or explore Extra Helpings.

Exam Week

➜ EXAMS
Answer question(s) related to the course.
Questions will come from:

Exam Week

➜ EXAMS
Answer question(s) related to the course.
Questions will come from:

Gideon's Battle
⍞ Materials: ESV Large Print Bible; Manners & Customs

➜ RECAP
What do you recall about what we read last time?

➜ INTRO
Gideon has his army put together to go up against the Midianites. Today we will read about that battle.

➜ READ & NARRATE
Judges 7:15b-18 (begin at "And he returned..."); 19-23; 8:22-27; 33-35
•How did the Israelites respond after Gideon died? (vs.33-35)

➜ READ & NARRATE
⍞ Materials: Manners & Customs p.17 Text box titled "The Priest's Clothing"
"In the ancient world around Israel, just as the gods were fed (sacrifices) and housed (temples) they were also clothed (that is, the images were). It is possible that Gideon's ephod represented the clothing of Yahweh (not on an actual image) just as the ark was the footstool of Yahweh's throne." (Bible Story Handbook) The ephod could be used as an oracle to discern God's will. In this sense, this particular ephod was idolatrous and inappropriate.

Optional: Turn back to p.12-13 to look at the images of typical clothing during Bible times.

The Cycle of Judges
⍞ Materials: ESV Large Print Bible; Notebook

➜ RECAP
What do you recall about what we read last time?

➜ INTRO
After Gideon, there was another 40 years of peace for the people of Israel. But sadly, the cycle begins again.

➜ READ & NARRATE
Judges 10:6-18
•When strong enemies came up against them the Israelites tended to turn toward those peoples' gods. What happens when they start worshipping these gods? (vs.6 "And they forsook the Lord and did not serve him.)
•Open your notebook to the drawing you did of the cycle of Israel in the book of Judges. What parts of today's reading fits into the chart?

The Birth of Samson
⍞ Materials: ESV Large Print Bible; Notebook

➜ RECAP
What do you recall about what we read last time?

➜ INTRO
The cycles in the book of Judges "show how God demonstrated his power and mercy by delivering them time after time after his justice had demanded that he bring punishment." Samson is the next judge of Israel and he is "worse than all the other judges as he pursued his own...personal agendas, yet God still used him to deliver the Israelites." (Bible Story Handbook) In the Bible, Samson, who we will meet today, and Samuel, who we will meet later in the year's readings, are the only two where the Nazirite vow extends to someone's entire lifetime. Typically, the vow was taken for a specific, limited time to show a high devotion to dedication.

➜ READ & NARRATE
Judges 13:1-20; 24-25

Honey from a Lion
⍞ Materials: ESV Large Print Bible; Notebook

➜ RECAP
What do you recall about what we read last time?

➜ INTRO
As we begin Judges 14, Samson is now grown up. He wants to marry a woman from the Philistines, but he is to be a deliverer of the Israelites, is it a good idea for him to marry a Philistine woman?

➜ READ & NARRATE
Judges 14:5-19
•"In ancient literature, kings and heroes were sometimes portrayed as killing a lion. While this episode fits a standard heroic feat, it also demonstrates Samson's disregard for his vow, as he did not hesitate to scoop honey from the corpse of the lion." (Bible Story Handbook) After reading this passage, what do you notice about Samson and how he acts or behaves? Does he fit your idea of a hero?

➜ NOTEBOOK
Draw a picture of today's passage or write a short summary. Older students may write a longer summary or copy down a couple main verses from the reading.

Samson's Revenge Against Philistines
⍞ Materials: ESV Large Print Bible; Manners & Customs

What do you recall about what we read last time?

Judges 15:1; 3-20

➜ READ & NARRATE
The passage from Manners & Customs in the Bible we will read today is a summary of warfare before there was a monarchy (kings over the entire nation of Israel), including some battles we have read about this year in Joshua, Judges and now into First Samuel.
⍞ Materials: Manners & Customs p.260-262 "Warfare is an"-"no standing army."

Preview today's passage for sensitive content regarding Samson's wife, her dad's offer of her sister, and the Philistine's subsequent reaction by burning up her and her father.

assignment_description:
Delilah Manipulates Samson
⍞ Materials: ESV Large Print Bible; Notebook

NOTE: The following note may be helpful if any questions arise from today's reading where in verse 15:20, it states that the "Lord had left him."
"In the Old Testament, the Spirit of the Lord was usually seen in empowering people to a task. Today we talk of the Spirit's indwelling people when they become Christians. These are very different things, and therefore this incident in Judges should not be viewed as the beginning of Samson's spiritual relationship with God. The endowment of the Spirit here had to do with his role as general." (Bible Story Handbook) Samson's choices are selfish, and yet God continues to use him as an instrument to accomplish His will.

➜ RECAP
What do you recall about what we read last time?

➜ INTRO
Samson meets Delilah, a woman who took a bribe (payment) from the Philistines to help weaken and defeat Samson. She accepts 5,500 shekels of silver, which - in that time - a typical wage would only be about 10 to 12 shekels for an entire year's worth of work! "And then what happened?"

➜ READ & NARRATE
Judges 16:4-31
•Delilah gets the best of Samson, but in v.28 Samson asks God to give him strength one more time in order to defeat the Philistines. What happens next? And why do you think God gave him strength in this situation?

➜ NOTEBOOK
Draw a picture or write a short summary of Samson and Delilah. Older students may write a longer summary, or copy down one or more important verses from this passage.

• EXTRA HELPING

Read p.150-151 in Manners & Customs of Bible Times to find out more about weaving and the loom and pin Delilah used to attempt to defeat Samson.

Micah
⍞ Materials: ESV Large Print Bible; Notebook

➜ RECAP
What do you recall about what we read last time?

➜ INTRO
Do you recall what genre of books these are that we have been reading? (Historical Narrative) Typically, Historical Narrative books answer the question "and then what happened?" However, in these final chapters of Judges, that pattern changes a bit. These stories are not what happened _next_, rather they tell stories of what happened _during_ the time of the judges we have been reading about.

In the final chapters of the book of Judges, no further judges are introduced. "No note of Israel's sin, of outside oppression, of a cry for help, or of God's provision for a hero-judge to save the people. These chapters are simply additional bits of history that the editor apparently did not want to leave out." (Basic Bible Commentary) The stories may have been used to show the moral decay the people had experienced during the time of the judges.

➜ READ & NARRATE
Let's read the first of the stories in these ending chapters. Micah, whose name (ironically) means "Who is like Yahweh?", is introduced in verse 1. In verse 2, he's going to tell his mom that the money stolen from her was actually taken by him. How do you think his mom will respond to this? Let's read to find out what this mom and son do next.

Judges 17:1-13
•How does the mother respond when Micah gives back the money? What does she make for him and his shrine of gods?
•Exodus 20:3-4: "You shall have no other gods before me. You shall not make for yourself a carved image, or any likeness of anything that is in heaven above, or that is in the earth beneath, or that is in the water under the earth." Are Micah and his mother obeying this rule in the law of Moses? Use information from today's reading to explain your answer. (They made a carved image and metal image; he already had a shrine with other gods.)
•Read 17:6, a key verse of the book of Judges, again. What do you imagine it would be like to live in a land where this was how people were living?

➜ RECAP
What do you recall about what we read last time?

➜ READ & NARRATE
Judges 18:1-13

"When the Jews"-"...breaking the basic commandments."

•Why do you think the author takes care to include the fact that the priests of the Danites set up Micah's idols at their new place in v.30-31?

Naomi and Ruth
⍞ Materials: ESV Large Print Bible; Notebook; Manners & Customs

➜ RECAP
What do you recall about what we read last time?

➜ INTRO
The book of Judges ends by repeating the sad statement one more time: "In those days there was no king in Israel. Everyone did what was right in his own eyes." Today we will begin the book of Ruth, which begins with a verse saying the story took place while the judges were ruling. Elimelech moves his family away from the city of Bethlehem to Moab because there is a famine. Moabites were an enemy of the people of Israel, but in that land, Ruth, a Moabite woman, marries into Naomi's family and shows faithfulness.

Ruth is a short, four-chapters-long book that shines in the midst of the darkness, because the story of Ruth holds hope. While the book of Judges showed us God can still work through people who were disobedient, foolish and selfish, the book of Ruth shows us what he can do through people who are faithful to him.

➜ READ, NARRATE & DISCUSS
Ruth 1:1-18
•"Whenever people were forced to leave the covenant Promised Land, it can be construed as either punishment or neglect of God. Since this story is in the judges period when the people were constantly unfaithful, we can infer that the famine was part of God's judgment. Drought and famine were included in the curses for violating the covenant (Deut. 28:23-24)." (Bible Story Handbook)
•What does Ruth choose to do in this passage? How is her loyalty and faithfulness different than what most people were choosing in the book of Judges?

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Materials: Manners & Customs
p.83 Locate the town of Bethlehem where Elimelech's family was from. Find Moab where the family moved and Ruth was from. What body of water lies between the two locations? (Salt Sea or Dead Sea)

➜ NOTEBOOK
Create a title page for the book of Ruth. Draw a picture or write a short summary of today's passage. Older students may write a longer summary or choose to copy down key verse(s) from the reading.

➜ READ & NARRATE

➜ READ & NARRATE

➜ INTRO

Manners & Customs "Winnowing"
p.96-97 "In the evening"-"...was not stolen."

Boaz the Redeemer
⍞ Materials: ESV Large Print Bible; Notebook

➜ RECAP
What do you recall about what we read last time?

➜ INTRO
In today's reading Boaz refers to himself as Ruth's "redeemer." In the ancient world, a widow's (someone whose husband has died) family would step in to help and provide security for the widow. "This included involvement with the remarriage of those who had been widowed and were without descendants, because descendants provided for each family in the tribe from generation to generation. This is how land was passed and held in the family." (Bible Story Handbook)

➜ READ & NARRATE
Ruth 3:6-4:6

➜ VIEW & DISCUSS
∞ Image Link: Boaz and Ruth by Gerbrand van den Eeckhout (1655)
View the image at the link. Does it match how you imagine the scene looked like when Boaz and Ruth met? Why or why not?

➜ NOTEBOOK
Draw a picture or write a short summary of what has been happening in the story of Ruth and Boaz. Older students could write a longer summary, or copy down one or more key verses from the passage.

Blessings
⍞ Materials: ESV Large Print Bible; Notebook

NOTE: Two options are given for the reading about city gates. Choose the one that best fits your student(s).

➜ RECAP
What do you recall about what we read last time?

➜ INTRO
Hope is seen in the redemptive story of Boaz and Ruth. Blessings come to them because they are following after God. God is faithful to follow through on his promises.

➜ READ & NARRATE
Ruth 4:7-22
•Do you recall when the book of Ruth took place? (Time of the judges.) At the beginning of the book, the family left Bethlehem because of a famine, but at the end of the book what are some evidences of the blessing Ruth and Boaz experienced because of the faithfulness of God? (Naomi holds her grandson on her lap; Ruth and Boaz are happily married; and some students may notice that King David is part of their family line in the future, which means they are also the in the genealogy of Jesus, our Redeemer.)

➜ READ & NARRATE
Manners & Customs
"The gate"
Younger students: p.175-176 "The gates were heavily defended"-"...always be open."
Older students, or for further information about gates of the city: p.174-176 "In the early"-"...always be open."

Hannah Prays
⍞ Materials: ESV Large Print Bible; Notebook

➜ RECAP
What do you recall about what we read last time?

➜ INTRO
The next two books we will read are First and Second Samuel. These two books continue in the genre of Historical Narrative and will tell what happens next as Israel begins a historical time period of moving from judges to kings. In I Samuel, we meet Israel's final judge named Samuel. The books are named after him, as he is a man who held "three offices: Prophet, priest, and judge." God will eventually use Samuel as an instrument to bring in the reign of King David. (Bible Story Handbook). In today's reading we will meet Hannah, who is Samuel's mother. She at first cannot have children, but God answers her prayer for a child because he is working to bring about his plan "to provide the king-maker (Samuel) of Israel through her."

➜ READ & NARRATE
I Samuel 1:1-28

➜ NOTEBOOK
Create a title page for the book of First Samuel, which you can write as: I Samuel, or as First Samuel. Draw a picture or write a short summary of today's reading. Older students can create a title page, draw a picture and write a longer summary of today's passage.

Hannah's Prayer & Eli's Sons
⍞ Materials: ESV Large Print Bible; Notebook

➜ RECAP
What do you recall about what we read last time?

➜ INTRO
Hannah has had a son and has committed him to the Lord's work. God had a divine reason and plan in mind to help the nation of Israel through Samuel. As he grows up, Samuel is serving in the temple and helping Eli the priest. Dressed in an ephod and robe, Samuel was most likely helping Eli personally, but also learning priestly tasks. This may have included "tasks carried out by the Levites. These included preparing animals for sacrifice and taking care of what was left after the sacrifice. There was also wood and water to be hauled." (Bible Story Handbook) Sadly, we will learn that Eli the priest had sons that were disobedient and Eli was not doing much to stop their misbehavior. Recall that this is still the time of the judges in the life of Israel, and we see how much that disobedience had corrupted every aspect of society, including the priests.

➜ READ & NARRATE
I Samuel 2:1-21

Eli's Sons
⍞ Materials: ESV Large Print Bible

➜ RECAP
What do you recall about what we read last time?

➜ INTRO
Eli's sons were corrupting the priestly office and Eli was not doing anything to stop it.

➜ READ & NARRATE
I Samuel 2:22-36
•What do you recall about what a priest's role (or job) was to do? (Represent the people before God; bring sacrifices, etc.) Why would this be a very important role?

Samuel's Call
⍞ Materials: ESV Large Print Bible; Manners & Customs; Notebook

➜ RECAP
What do you recall about what we read last time?

➜ INTRO
While Eli's sons and Eli are struggling (and failing) to keep the priesthood holy, Samuel continues to grow older. In today's reading, Samuel will receive a special call from the Lord.

➜ READ & NARRATE
First, we will read from Manners & Customs about what a prophet in Old Testament times did and what his role was.
Younger students: p.330-331 "A person became"-"and in truth."
Older students: "The prophets" p.328-331 "Alongside the priests"-"and in truth."

➜ READ & NARRATE
I Samuel 3:1-21

➜ NOTEBOOK
Draw a picture or write a summary of the passage. Older students may write a longer summary, or copy down a verse or more from today's reading. The student may also record any notes of interest regarding what a prophet's role was at the time of I Samuel.

Philistines Capture the Ark
⍞ Materials: ESV Large Print Bible; Manners & Customs

PREP: Two parts of the story can be added in as appropriate for your student(s):
1. "The story of the ark brings an end to the judges period of history, as God's ultimate judgment on the people was to abandon them to their oppressors. He did this by leaving the land and bringing judgement on the Philistines without raising up a judge."
2. A note on the god Dagon and the ark: "The first time Dagon fell in front of the ark, it was as though he was bowing before God." The second time, "his head and arms fell off, which is an "indication that he had been slain in battle. In the ancient world enemy casualties were sometimes counted by making a pile of the heads or hand of the slain. The destruction of Dagon was a very visible way for Yahweh to show the Philistines that he had not been defeated." (Bible Story Handbook)

➜ RECAP
What do you recall about what we read last time?

➜ INTRO
The ark of the covenant is captured by the Philistines in the chapters we will read from today. Let's look at some information about the ark of the covenant before reading the passage.

Manners & Customs
1. Turn to p.301 and look at the image of the ark of the covenant based on the description from passages in the Bible. While nobody knows where the ark is located currently, artists have drawn or tried to show what it possibly looked like. Take a moment to look at the image of the priest as well. Do you remember what the name of the overlay he is wearing is called? (An ephod.)
2. Read p.331-332 "Holy objects"
Younger students: p.331 "In the Jewish"-"served the same purpose."
Older students, or as time and interest allow: p.331-333 "In the Jewish"-"of the house." View the image of the priest on p.333. What do you notice about what he is
holding, reading and wearing?

➜ READ & NARRATE
I Samuel 4:1-11; 5:1-11
•The Philistines captured the ark and put it before their god, Dagon. What happened next?
